title: Your Books Are Accurate. That's Not the Same as Decision-Ready.
linkedin:
Accurate books and decision-ready numbers are not the same thing.

We see this constantly across owner-led businesses. The BAS is lodged on time. The bank reconciles. The annual accounts are clean. By every measure that matters to a compliance accountant, the business is in good shape.

And yet the owner still can't say, without a week of exports, which service line actually made money last quarter. Or whether the new hire has paid for themselves. Or whether growth should be funded from cash or from a facility.

That's not a bookkeeping problem. Compliance accounting was built to answer one question: did the numbers happen the way they were recorded. It was never built to answer a forward question about pricing, hiring or cash timing.

The test that cuts through most of the ambiguity: can the business answer, within a day, what its most profitable service line was last quarter and why. If that takes a scramble, the books are accurate. They're just not decision-ready yet.

That gap doesn't show up as a single bad number. It shows up quietly, month after month, in decisions made on instinct that a forward model would have made obvious.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#FractionalCFO #SMEFinance #BusinessOwners
facebook:
Quick question for the business owners here: can you say, right now, which part of your business made the most money last quarter? Not guess. Actually know, without a week of digging through spreadsheets.

Most owners can't, and it's not because their books are wrong. The BAS is lodged, the accounts are clean, everything reconciles. Accurate books just weren't built to answer that kind of question. They were built to answer "did this happen correctly," not "what should happen next."

That gap is where a lot of quiet, avoidable cost hides.

Where does your business sit on this one?

#SMEFinance
google:
A note for business owners across Brisbane, Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria: accurate books and decision-ready numbers are two different things, and it's worth knowing which one your business actually has.

Lodging on time, reconciling the bank account and getting clean annual accounts back each year are all signs the compliance side of the business is well looked after. That work matters and it's not the part that's missing for most owners.

What it doesn't do is tell you which part of the business made the most money last quarter, whether a recent hire has paid for itself, or whether the next growth step should come from cash in the bank or a facility. Those are commercial questions, and compliance accounting was never designed to answer them.

A simple check: can you answer, within a day and without a scramble, what your most profitable service line was last quarter and why. If that takes a week of digging, the books are accurate but not yet decision-ready, and that gap tends to cost more than most owners realise.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
